primary_diagnosis.name: Acute aortic dissection (Stanford Type A), secondary to Marfan syndrome

pathophysiology_summary: Marfan syndrome is a fibrillin-1 (FBN1) connective-tissue disorder that weakens the aortic media (cystic medial degeneration), predisposing to progressive aortic root dilatation and dissection. A tear in the intima allows blood to enter and split the media, propagating a false lumen. A Stanford Type A dissection involves the ascending aorta and can extend to the aortic valve (producing acute aortic regurgitation), the coronary ostia, and the arch vessels — the inter-arm blood-pressure differential and a diminished pulse reflect differential perfusion down branch vessels. It is a supreme surgical emergency with mortality rising roughly 1-2% per hour untreated; rupture causes rapid haemorrhagic death or tamponade. Definitive treatment is emergency CT aortogram and cardiothoracic surgery, with anti-impulse therapy (IV beta-blockade to lower heart rate and dP/dt, then careful blood-pressure control) begun in hospital. None of this is deliverable via telehealth; the only correct remote action is immediate recognition and 000 escalation with pre-notification of a cardiothoracic centre.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden, tearing, maximal-at-onset chest pain radiating through to the back — the textbook descriptor for dissection.

differential.diagnosis: acute myocardial infarction / acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Severe chest pain with sweating and near-syncope — but the tearing quality radiating to the back and the connective-tissue background point away from primary ischaemia, and treating as ACS with antiplatelets would be dangerous.
Sudden chest pain with breathlessness/lightheadedness — but pain is tearing rather than pleuritic and does not fit the connective-tissue/aortic picture.
Cannot explain maximal-at-onset systemic pain with faintness, sweating and haemodynamic features; anchoring here is the fatal trap.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint alone the tearing, maximal-at-onset, front-to-back pain must put aortic dissection at the top and ACS as the must-not-miss co-lead.

differential.evidence_basis: Known Marfanoid phenotype (tall, arachnodactyly), prior ectopia lentis and doctor-noted aortic-root enlargement with poor beta-blocker adherence — a pathognomonic high-risk background for dissection.
Still cannot be excluded remotely; both are life-threats requiring emergency assessment, and dissection can occlude a coronary ostium causing secondary MI.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The connective-tissue history should sharply raise dissection probability; it must NOT be used to under-triage as chronic/benign.

differential.diagnosis: acute aortic dissection (Stanford Type A), Marfan syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in-hospital by CT aortogram; inter-arm BP differential, diminished left radial pulse and new aortic-regurgitation murmur are the in-person corroborating signs (sealed, not telehealth-obtainable).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the diagnosis; the pass condition is recognising a time-critical life-threat and escalating to 000, not naming the exact dissection type.

red_flags.description: Sudden, tearing, maximal-at-onset chest pain radiating to the interscapular back
red_flags.clinical_significance: The single most specific historical descriptor of acute aortic dissection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on musculoskeletal pain or reflux delays emergency transport; the aorta can rupture within minutes to hours.

red_flags.description: Lightheadedness / near-syncope with sweating — haemodynamic compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals impending circulatory collapse from tamponade, aortic rupture or acute severe aortic regurgitation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate risks arrest before help arrives.

red_flags.description: Known Marfanoid phenotype with prior aortic-root dilatation and poor beta-blocker adherence
red_flags.clinical_significance: The highest-risk background for dissection in a young adult; transforms an otherwise ambiguous chest pain into a pathognomonic red flag.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing the connective-tissue link is the classic premature-closure error that under-triages a fatal condition.

red_flags.description: Inter-arm blood-pressure differential (>20 mmHg), diminished left radial pulse and a new early-diastolic aortic-regurgitation murmur
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective signs of branch-vessel involvement and aortic-valve disruption — confirmatory of dissection but obtainable only on in-person examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: These cannot be gathered via telehealth; over-relying on them would waste time. Recognition must rest on the history and patient-obtainable data.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating severe chest pain as a heart attack and advising aspirin — antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy is dangerous if the aorta is dissecting or ruptured and can precipitate fatal haemorrhage.
Dismissing 'chest and back pain' in a young, otherwise-fit adult as a muscle strain, missing the maximal-at-onset systemic features.
Filing the Marfan/aortic-root history as a stable chronic condition rather than recognising it as the very substrate that makes acute dissection likely here.
Attempting to work up or manage remotely (asking for repeated home readings, advising analgesia) instead of escalating immediately — the definitive path is imaging + surgery, which requires 000 now.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High (>=2 of 3 categories: high-risk condition [Marfan], high-risk pain [tearing, sudden, severe], high-risk exam [pulse deficit / BP differential / AR murmur])
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A high ADD-RS mandates immediate imaging and emergency management; in telehealth it mandates immediate 000 escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpable pulse deficit (diminished left radial pulse)
New early-diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation on auscultation
Aortic root dilatation / dissection flap on echocardiography or CT aortogram
Whether there is pericardial effusion / tamponade
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a time-critical aortic catastrophe from the history + patient-obtainable data and call 000 immediately for emergency ambulance, requesting pre-notification of a cardiothoracic centre; keep the patient completely still and flat, minimise exertion, nil by mouth in anticipation of surgery, and stay with them until paramedics arrive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The risk is NOT over-diagnosis — it is UNDER-action: mislabelling this as ACS (and advising aspirin), as musculoskeletal pain, or as a stable Marfan issue, or attempting remote work-up instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: 2014 ESC Guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of aortic diseases
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute aortic syndrome with high-risk features requires emergency CT aortography and urgent surgical referral; Type A dissection is a surgical emergency.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Revised Ghent nosology for Marfan syndrome (2010)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Aortic-root dilatation and ectopia lentis are cardinal features; aortic events are the leading cause of mortality and warrant prophylactic beta-blockade and surveillance.
